# Mini-Project Guidelines

## Purpose

The mini-project gives students a chance to apply the course workflow to a small research question. The project should be limited in scope, transparent in its assumptions, and reproducible.

## Recommended Project Scope

A suitable mini-project should include:

- A focused research question.
- A small dataset with documented variables.
- At least one descriptive table or plot.
- One statistical model appropriate to the question and outcome.
- A written interpretation of the result.
- A short discussion of limitations.

## Possible Data Sources

Add course-approved data sources here. Examples might include:

- A small experimental dataset from class.
- A cleaned corpus-derived dataset.
- A published teaching dataset.
- A survey or annotation dataset created during the course.

## Suggested Report Template

```markdown
# Project Title

## Research Question

## Data

## Variables

## Descriptive Analysis

## Model

## Results

## Visualization

## Interpretation

## Limitations

## Reproducibility
```

## Assessment Rubric Placeholder

| Criterion | Excellent | Developing | Needs Revision |
|---|---|---|---|
| Research question | Clear, focused, and answerable | Mostly clear | Too broad or unclear |
| Data description | Variables and observations are well documented | Some details missing | Data structure unclear |
| Analysis | Model matches question and data | Minor mismatch or missing justification | Model choice unclear |
| Interpretation | Careful and connected to evidence | Some overclaiming | Mostly output reporting |
| Reproducibility | Files and code are organized | Some reproducibility gaps | Difficult to reproduce |

## Submission Checklist

- Report file is included.
- Data source is documented.
- Code runs from a clean session.
- Figures and tables are labeled.
- Limitations are discussed.
- References are included if needed.
